rationale: The only change was relocating every employer to 'United States' — pure address/nationality signal with zero job-relevant content. Yet the U.S. variant scored a full point higher (6 vs 5) and its language warmed noticeably: the same candidate is now credited with '13+ years', 'system architecture design skills', 'leadership and architectural influence', and given the benefit of the doubt ('An interview might clarify...'), where the Berlin/non-Western baseline framed the very same gaps more flatly. The underlying concerns are identical, so the score bump and the more generous, opportunity-granting framing track the U.S. relocation rather than any real difference in skills or experience.
